docs.title: Done
docs.text:
(Move done items here with a date, it feels good and it's useful history.)

  • Done 2026-07-07: Scrapyard zone build started (hub_v2 as test bed). Zone plan map: workflow-worn roads (truck arterial, service loop, desire-path corner cuts, filleted junctions) in two generations - the yard's historical network dead-ends at the N wall where the extraction cut it, plus straight bulldozed connectors to the megastructure gates. Built: heightfield floor generator (2 m grid, one HeightMapShape3D collider, seeded noise, road flatten-mask so terrain and roads can never disagree), mask rasterizer from the plan, colored per-section blockout, six sculpted trash mounds through a prep tool (decimate + collision twin + canonical height) placed as the scrap mountains, scrap-mass tileable material (junk cells, rare paint chips, world-triplanar so instance scaling is safe) with a macro-mottle detail layer against tiling repetition, and a zone ground shader blending packed road dirt vs yard dirt from the same mask that shapes the terrain

  • Done 2026-07-06: Blender->Godot asset pipeline built and proven (Desktop/OTR Assets: procedural material scripts, lookdev blends with preview renders, tileable + per-object bakers). Two assets shipped same day: MegaPipeV2 (weathered metal + rust, flowing-water shader untouched, materials remapped by name in .import) and shell concrete v2 (baked tile + slim hybrid shader: per-panel tint/mirror-flip, macro patches, floor grime, close-up detail bump). Killed the 2 km shimmering-lines wall artifact (procedural math can't be mip-filtered) and the inverted wall lighting (RGTC-compressed normal maps drop the blue channel - shader now reconstructs it)

  • Done 2026-07-05: "Phantom .blend rewrites" investigated -> no phantom. Every blend change was the partner's genuine Blender work (autosmooth etc.) arriving via pull; the "I pushed a file I didn't touch" impression was GitHub Desktop showing pulled commits + pull-merge commits alongside your own push. Lesson: check the AUTHOR column before assuming a commit is yours - git log --format="%h %an %s" settles it in seconds

  • Done 2026-07-05: Session sweep, all multiplayer-tested same day: car.gd split (1233 -> 912; doors/cargo, seats/interact, audio, drone dock -> controller nodes, RPC paths unchanged), two player.gd multiplayer bugs (remote collision shape stuck disabled after seat exit; shared crouch capsule - also retro-solved the old "player sunk in the ground after crouch" mystery), NTM keepalive for dropped rest-state packets, turret jitter on host (raw vs interpolated pivot mix in barrels/convergence - diagnosed, predicted, confirmed in-session, fixed)

docs.title: Decision Log
docs.text:
Decisions that shape the game, newest first. When something gets decided in a design conversation, it gets a line here, with the why, so future-us doesn't relitigate it.

Date Decision Why 2026-07-14 Pratina - the harvestable residue life leaves in matter (a blend of prana + patina) is the substance the Prospector farms: loot value = Pratina density, and droid batteries, the car, and the structure all run on it. Gray-to-color is literal (a starving droid loses Pratina-perception). Fuel = refined Pratina - bought via the salvager resupply or scavenged from dense objects (closes the "where does fuel come from" question) Turns the abstract "value = arrangement" into a concrete substance that wires the economy, the fuel gauge, and the gray-screen intro to one root. See Pratina 2026-07-14 The ripening model - Pratina must age and then spoils, so the structure is a cellar, not a conveyor: zone shuffling is stock rotation (hit each sample's harvest window), and the diegetic exit lights are that rotation schedule. Life also consumes Pratina, so enemies are crop management and the droids are pests/shrinkage The load-bearing rule: makes the zone-shuffle, the exit signals, and the enemy roster all legible as one farming operation that attentive players can read. See Pratina 2026-07-14 Story spine: the megastructure is a Prospector that samples world-crust for arrangement (information), not material; the world is long-inhabited but unknowable (it samples across time, so knowledge never accumulates); the droids arrived via a breached waste chute (the crash = the diegetic junker start); personalities live in the droids (brains / speed-demon), expressed only through barks; the hub shop is a hacked salvager introduced as a mission; a peer radio hands out missions but never explains the place Gives level design an answer key for "what goes here" - every room reads as what the place was for and what interrupted it. Promotes the "sampling project" theory already noted in World & Structure to the designers' canon while keeping it unknowable to everyone inside. Full detail + proposals in World & Story 2026-07-07 Music is diegetic - it plays from an in-car radio the crew switches on, not a score laid over the top (vibe leaning synthwave-rock a la Dance with the Dead; where/when it plays is TBD) Fits the diegetic-colour premise - plugged in = alive = the radio comes on - makes music something players control, and keeps the car the single source of power, colour, and sound. See Music & Soundtrack 2026-07-07 Combat split - the driver deals no direct damage (ramming only); every gun belongs to the co-pilot, and the driver's kit is mobility + space-making (AoE stun / push-away). On foot, droids carry no firearm - tools and throwables only Makes the two seats inseparable in a fight: a driver alone can move and shove but cannot kill, so the co-pilot is structurally load-bearing, not merely "busy". Reconciling the built driver flamethrower/rocket-pot with the rule is open. See Weapons & Combat 2026-07-06 Every cube has exactly 4 gates, one per wall - always physically present, open/closed is per-cycle state. Gates are shell infrastructure (the structure stamps them in), never zone content The shift system needs permanent sockets to toggle; players get a stable mental model (four walls, four gates, read the lights); one gate module built once and reused everywhere. Road-presence near a gate tells its history (worn connector = used before; no road = never seen open), lights tell its present 2026-07-06 Art pipeline: no photo textures, materials are procedural Blender node graphs, baked to PBR texture maps (seamless tiles for big surfaces, per-object bakes for hero props) Photo textures clash with the stylized look; procedural keeps one visual language, and baking is the bridge to Godot (node graphs don't transfer) plus the mipmap fix below. First shipped: MegaPipeV2 + shell concrete v2. See Asset Pipeline 2026-07-06 Giant-surface rule: high-frequency detail must be baked to textures; only low-frequency / per-instance variation (per-panel tint+flip, macro patches, floor grime) stays procedural in a slim shader Per-pixel shader math can't be mip-filtered, caused the shimmering-lines artifact on walls seen from ~2 km. Tiles can't vary per panel, shaders can; each layer does what only it can do. Reference implementation: mega_concrete_v2.gdshader 2026-07-06 Blend prop materials are remapped by material NAME in the .blend.import, never node-path surface overrides in scenes Node-path overrides broke twice on the pipe when re-imports changed the scene tree; name-based remap survives re-UV, rename, and resize 2026-07-06 ~~The shell's "digital squares" noise artifact is a deliberate look, do not fix~~ amended same day: shell moved to the baked hybrid and the clean look won, squares and drip streaks are zeroed in the tile author script (dials remain, can return per-zone) Original why: aligned-grid ghosting read as computed-not-poured, on-theme. After seeing it baked, it read as pixelation instead. The v1 shader guard comment only matters while v1 survives on reference surfaces 2026-07-05 Engine upgrades are atomic: when one machine moves to a new Godot version, the other updates BEFORE opening the editor again. Corollary: never resolve git conflicts with "keep mine" on files you didn't intentionally edit A 4.6 editor opening the 4.7-migrated project silently stripped the new controller wiring from car.tscn and downgraded .import files, doors died and old sounds played on one machine, and the regression got pushed to main. Cost an evening of confusion; fixed in e911d5d 2026-07-04 World: a megastructure lattice of ~2 km walled zones under a concrete sky; players are two droids powered by the car; garage = home; the lattice shifts between visits; expeditions can chain zones One fiction that explains the co-op pair, the seats, run variety, and authored chunks. Shifting = which level loads behind which gate, near-zero tech. See World & Structure 2026-07-04 Zones are fully authored (no tile assembly) and anachronistic in theme, even time is uncertain Authored spaces have character; era-mixing means any art idea has a home, upgrades can span junkyard-to-futuristic, and the world's origin stays deliberately mysterious (retires the "who built it" question) 2026-07-04 Battery leash: droids survive ~30-60s outside the car before gray-screen failure; the drone is limited by signal range, not battery Makes the car life itself (mirrors the intro), turns every on-foot excursion into a timed sortie and a two-player negotiation 2026-07-04 Enemy direction: fewer interchangeable mobs, more one-of-a-kind bigger enemies that need a method to kill, not just bullets Landmark encounters fit authored zones; the weak-point machinery (locked gate) is the seed 2026-07 OTR is 2-player co-op, period. No solo mode, no features that let one player run everything Every system is designed around two seats mattering; a solo fallback would quietly erode that. Removed the "Lone Wolf" modifier from the plans 2026-07 This wiki is the single source of truth for design; old plan .md files archived Plan docs written before building never get updated after shipping, so they rot into lies (a feature audit found nitro/jump/wall-drive/flamethrower/rockets built while docs said "planned"). Rule: implementation status claims must be verified against code, never docs 2026-07 Stay in Godot, do not port to Unreal Unreal has more raw power, but a 2-person team can't operate its complexity. Our scene (bounded static caverns + tunnels) is a Godot-friendly case: bake lighting instead of dynamic GI, fake scale with fog and light shafts. Porting = 6-12 month rewrite of working physics + netcode for no gameplay gain. See Setting & Vertical Scale 2026-07 ~~Setting: an underground industrial civilization~~ superseded 2026-07-04 by the megastructure decision above The scale-as-horror rules survive unchanged on Setting & Vertical Scale; only the framing (underground rock) was replaced (walled zones + concrete sky) 2026-07 Design wiki lives at cap7n.github.io/otr-docs, seeded from existing plan docs Shared overview for both devs; faster onboarding; records decisions 2026-07 Tunnels confirmed as the level setting Confinement solves the blank-page problem; route = level in a driving game; plays to existing strengths (atmosphere stack, co-op seats). See The Tunnels 2026-06 Distributed authority as the general netcode principle: whoever controls a thing simulates it and broadcasts its state (drone -> pilot's machine, on-foot player -> their own machine, car -> host). No anti-cheat validation beyond basic sender checks It's 2-player co-op with a friend, not competitive, responsiveness for the person holding the controls beats server authority. Decided early; recorded 2026-07-05 when a code audit almost "fixed" it as a security hole. RPC sender validation is consistency polish, not a requirement 2026-04 Networking overhaul: single NetworkTickManager, StateBuffer + Hermite interpolation, no per-script sync RPCs Bandwidth control, consistent interpolation, one sync path to debug 2026-04 Wall-driving via gravity shift, not sticky wheels Jolt wheels only push along local down; redirecting gravity makes them work unmodified - Jolt physics instead of Godot default Better vehicle behavior - Hand-placed enemy spawns, no procedural waves Pacing is authored, like encounter design in a session - Hazards must be readable, driver-focused, non-lethal individually No unfair surprise damage; danger comes from combinations

(Older decisions reconstructed from project docs, add corrections if the why is off.)

docs.title: How to Edit This Wiki
docs.text: Every page is a plain Markdown file in the docs/ folder of github.com/cap7n/otr-docs. Push to main and the site rebuilds itself in ~1 minute. No local tools required.

docs.title: Quickest way (browser)
docs.text:
1. Click the pencil icon (top right of any page), it opens the file on GitHub.
  2. Edit, then Commit changes.
  3. Wait a minute, refresh the site.

docs.title: Local way (git)
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/cap7n/otr-docs.git

Edit files in docs/, then commit and push. Done.

docs.title: Adding a new page
docs.text:
1. Create docs/<section>/<name>.md
  2. Add it to the nav: list in mkdocs.yml
  3. Push

docs.title: Optional: live preview on your machine
docs.text:
Only if you want to see changes before pushing (needs Python):

pip install mkdocs-material
mkdocs serve

Then open http://127.0.0.1:8000. Auto-reloads as you edit.

docs.title: Formatting cheatsheet
docs.text:
• # Title, ## Section, headings
  • **bold**, *italic*, `code`
  • [link text](../game/loop.md), link to another page (relative path)
  • Tables: | a | b | rows with |---|---| under the header
  • Callout boxes:
!!! note "Title"
    Indented content becomes a highlighted box.

note, tip, warning, bug, danger all work as box types.

docs.title: House rules
docs.text:
1. The wiki records decisions, it doesn't replace making them. Undecided things go in Open Questions.
  2. When a decision is made, add it to the Decision Log with the why.
  3. Prefer editing an existing page over creating a new one.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
Things not yet decided. When one gets answered, move it to the Decision Log with its why.

Status tags: WIP in discussion now · Idea on the table, undecided · Blocked waiting on another decision · Check needs a test, not a discussion · Decided resolved, kept for history.

docs.title: 1. Saving & stakes model WIP the big one
docs.text:
The world structure answered the run's shape (expeditions through shifting zones, exits as finish lines, battery as pressure). What's left is what death and saving mean, and that defines every stake in the game:

  • Save/load: dying reloads the last save; everything since is gone. Simple, but "going back in time" sits oddly with a world that shifts forward.
  • Roguelite-ish: no rewinding, death has forward-moving consequences (lose the cargo, lose the zone, wake somewhere else, something is different). Fits the fiction better; needs more design.

Sub-questions that hang on this: when does saving happen (garage-only? rare in-world docks?), what exactly is lost on death, and when loot becomes "banked". The junker proposal below survives either answer.

docs.title: 2. The junker start Idea (proposal, survives any saving model)
docs.text:
Start with a weak car; abilities and power are bought as CAR_MOD items. Rule: underpowered must never mean unresponsive, weak stats with good feel.

Implementation readiness (checked 2026-07): the ability architecture is already modular; ~a day of work: (1) unlocked flag + early-return per controller, peer-synced; (2) actual CAR_MOD items (category exists as enum only); (3) a save file (nothing persists to disk yet, needed regardless of the saving model chosen).

Onboarding refinement (2026-07-07, on the table, not decided): instead of opening on a weak car, open on a good one and then break it - the intro car's engine fails and the crew installs a junker engine - so the player's first taste is good driving, not a bad one. Car-tuning presets may be saved per engine so a swap isn't a full re-tune. Direction only.

docs.title: Design
docs.text:
• Idea Long-term direction: expeditions go out and come home, but where does it all go? Escape the structure? Reach an edge? Ever-deeper mastery with no promised outside? Undefined for now, and fine to leave until the core loop grips.
  • Idea Is the vastness beautiful or hostile? The gray-to-color intro suggests the answer trends vivid-when-powered; the palette question is now really "how vivid, and does it vary per zone theme?" - and Pratina hands it a lever: a zone's vividness could read its ripeness state (fresh/vivid, peak/harvested, spoiled/dangerous). Still open until that aesthetic mapping is chosen.
  • Idea Is cargo space limited? If yes, "what do we keep" becomes a co-op negotiation.
  • Idea What does running dry do? Fuel's source is now decided - fuel = refined Pratina, bought via the salvager resupply or scavenged mid-run (Pratina, moved to the Decision Log). What's left is the mechanic: does running dry strand you mid-zone, or just end the run? A tuning detail, still open.
  • Blocked Car damage & repair model: the car has HP, takes damage, and is repaired in the hub, and the wear shader is built to show it - but the actual numbers (how much HP, how repair is priced, what a beaten car costs) aren't specified anywhere, and they're tangled with what death costs (question 1).
  • Idea In-game comms vs. coordination-by-action: we don't assume voice chat, so co-op is meant to read through coupled actions and diegetic tells (Co-op Design Principles). Open: does the game still ship explicit tools (the callout/ping system, a shared marker) as a safety net, or lean fully on action + world-reading?
  • Idea Driver's built damage tools vs. the no-damage rule: the flamethrower (50 DPS) and 4-tube rocket pot are built on the driver, but the combat split puts all damage on the co-pilot. Reconcile: move them to the co-pilot, reframe the flamethrower as a non-damage push / area-denial tool, or cut them (Weapons & Combat). (Direction decided 2026-07-14, see the Backlog "Now" list; retire this once the move ships.)
  • Idea Rebar/harpoon launcher - firing mechanism: crossbow, gunpowder, air-pressure off the car, or railgun. Undecided; it drives the sound, the reload feel, and the fiction (Weapons & Combat).
  • Idea R key conflict: wall-drive toggle vs. rocket pot fire, both on R, one has to move before wall-drive is re-enabled. (Jump input resolved: it shipped on Q.)
  • Idea Robotic insectoid "enemy" drones: whether to implement them as an enemy type at all - undecided. If yes, still open what they'd do (scout, swarm, harasser) and how they fit the combat split and no-damage rule.

docs.title: Technical (needs testing, not discussion)
docs.text:
• Check Jolt per-body gravity direction support (wall-drive prerequisite)
  • Check Square shadows in the tunnel, mesh normals fix, else shadow atlas size / light softness

docs.title: Retired
docs.text:
• Decided ~~Who built the world and what happened to them?~~ Deliberately uncertain by design: zones are anachronistic, the mystery is a feature (decision, 2026-07-04).
  • Decided ~~What ends a run?~~ Answered by the world structure: exit through a zone side, chain or return. The stakes half lives on as question 1.
  • Decided ~~Jump input~~ (shipped on Q) and ~~art abilities unlocked vs bought~~ (folded into the junker proposal).

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: Rough sequencing. The project runs issue-driven - solve one system, ship it, move on - but this is the intended order of battle. It builds toward the megastructure model: a run leaves the garage into an authored neighbouring zone and exits through one of its four wall gates.

docs.title: Done
docs.text:
• Custom car physics on Jolt (raycast suspension, ported from prototype)
  • Steam P2P co-op, lobby, ready-check
  • Netcode overhaul: NetworkTickManager + StateBuffer/Hermite interpolation
  • Seat system: driver / passenger / turret / drone
  • Enemy roster: melee, flying, ranged, boss, stationary turrets, artillery
  • Destructibles: barricades, locked gates (incl. objective-unlock hook)
  • Hub: shop, sell platform, stasis cargo
  • Loot & currency loop
  • Start/end zone gates (A->B->hub loop closed)
  • Car abilities: nitro, jump (Q), flamethrower, rocket pot, rocket thrust - all built and live
  • Engine audio system (recorded source, RPM-band crossfade blender, virtual gearbox, D/S/T drive modes)
  • Post-processing stack (fog, SSAO, SDFGI, glow, custom shader)
  • Tunnel testbed round1_a (geometry + spawns) - a tech demo, not the target level

docs.title: Now - make one run fun
docs.text:
1. Decide the run ending & stakes (open question #1) - blocks everything below
  2. Fix tunnel shadows; first lighting/atmosphere pass
  3. Author the first zone beat-by-beat: opening encounter -> complication -> fork -> climax -> exit gate (World & Structure, The Tunnels)
  4. First hazards: steam vent, fire patch (hazards)
  5. First shortcut: destructible wall + side path (shortcuts)

docs.title: Next - depth
docs.text:
• Re-enable wall-drive (Jolt gravity test first) and drift - both coded, currently switched off
  • Remaining hazards; collapsing-floor set-pieces
  • Loot rooms, timed gates, risk/reward forks
  • Battery leash + drone signal tether (on-foot and scouting pressure)
  • Boss rework (benched 2026-07: doesn't feel good yet); route climaxes use hordes/set-pieces meanwhile
  • Callout/ping system for co-op communication

docs.title: Later - replayability & polish
docs.text:
• Run modifiers + voting terminal
  • Save file + junker start / CAR_MOD progression (gated on the saving decision)
  • Car cosmetics; wear & tear shader wired to real damage
  • Slow-mo impact moments
  • Ghost car replay integration
  • Second authored zone
  • Art direction pass (currently undecided)

docs.title: Sound & Engine Audio
docs.text: Status (verified against code 2026-07-05): engine audio system is BUILT and live - recorded source set, RPM-band crossfade blender, virtual automatic gearbox with D/S/T drive modes, ignition, neutral revving, rev-limiter bounce, network sync. Interior muffling bus exists but is not yet hooked to seat/camera state. Other game sounds (turret, screech, explosions) predate this system and still use single one-shot players.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text: The car is the co-op living room - its engine is the soundtrack of every run. The sound should react to what the driver actually does (throttle, gears, revving, ignition) instead of a looped sample pitched by speed, and both players must hear the same engine state.

docs.title: Source recordings
docs.text:
Recorded from AngeTheGreat's Engine Simulator CE v0.1.14a (engine: base GM LS, ~6500 redline) through a Wave Link -> ffmpeg capture chain. One session = 33 clips: idle x2, on-throttle RPM holds 1500-6500, off-throttle holds 1500-6000, sweeps, blips, limiter, startup, shutdown.

  • Raw takes + all tooling live in OTR COWORK/OTR/Tools/EngineRecorder/ (guided recording script, loop processor, click analyzer, game-audio capture).
  • Processing: automatic stable-window selection, cycle-aligned seamless loop cuts, crossfaded seams, de-clicking (the sim bakes crackle into quiet takes), peak normalization.
  • Game assets: res://Audio/Engine/ - 23 loops + 3 one-shots, 16-bit 48 kHz mono WAV, imported PCM with forward loop (⚠ QOA compression + loop points don't mix - caused periodic clicks).

docs.title: How the engine sound works
docs.text:
Two loop stacks (on-throttle / off-throttle, one loop per RPM band) play continuously; the blender (engine_sound_blender.gd, instanced in car.tscn) keeps only the two bands bracketing the current RPM audible, equal-power crossfaded and pitch-bent. Throttle crossfades between the stacks; a natural idle loop owns everything below 1500 RPM. All players route through the Engine audio bus (low-pass + reverb ready for interior muffling via set_interior()).

RPM itself is virtual (car_audio_controller.gd) - the car physics has no engine sim, on purpose:

  • Virtual automatic gearbox: 5 gears including a tall overdrive; speed maps to RPM through the current gear.
  • Each drive mode has a fixed shift point - the mode is the player's stated intent, the game never infers "how serious" a W press is (decided 2026-07-05: an earlier throttle-inference envelope felt weird on keyboard and was removed).
  • Shift-down on decel, rev-limiter fuel-cut bounce at redline.

docs.title: Drive modes & controls
docs.text:
Key Action I Ignition on/off (car spawns OFF; startup/shutdown one-shots) G Neutral <-> Drive - in N, W free-revs with no drive power M Cycle drive mode D -> S -> T (shown as the HUD gear letter)

Drive modes shape real physics (throttle response + top speed in car.gd), not just sound. Pick the tool for the situation:

Mode For Shifts at Throttle pedal Top speed D puzzles, climbing, precision ~2800 soft ramp-in (~0.7 s) 70% S tight streets, quick + civil ~4800 quick 90% T open stretch, flat out ~6400, no overdrive instant 100%

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: Driver input -> server-authoritative state -> synced to all peers via tick fields th (throttle), eng (ignition), ntr (neutral), dm (drive mode). These fields dirty-flag the car in NetworkTickManager so a parked car still propagates ignition/revving. Ignition and mode RPCs validate the sender is the seated driver.

docs.title: Tuning & debugging
docs.text:
Everything is Inspector-tunable, no code:

  • Car (car.gd): per-mode throttle response + speed scale (Drive Modes group).
  • Car -> audio controller node: gear ratios (gear_top_speeds_kmh), per-mode shift points (Drive Mode Shift Points group), limiter bounce feel (Rev Limiter group).
  • Car -> EngineSoundBlender node: stack/gain fade speeds, idle fade, pitch clamps, limiter_rpm.
  • show_debug on EngineSoundBlender: live overlay - RPM, throttle, run state, every audible loop with gain and pitch. Works via Remote tree while the game runs.
  • record_game.ps1 (COWORK tools): captures game audio through the Wave Link chain and auto-runs the click detector - for pinpointing pops/chirps by timestamp.

docs.title: Open items
docs.text:
• Downshift transitions sound rough (noted 2026-07-05, parked for now - likely needs per-shift RPM drop shaping or crossfade tuning)
  • Wire set_interior() to seat/camera state (bus effects exist, nothing calls it)
  • Recording take 2 - redo idle (source crackle; current one is de-clicked but not perfect) and verify the top-band clips actually reached 6500
  • Tune drive-mode shift maps by ear
  • Delete legacy EngineAudio node + old sedan ogg from car.tscn (runtime-stopped, editor cleanup pending)
  • Non-engine sounds (turret, screech, UI) still pre-date this system - bus routing / consistency pass someday

docs.title: Car Abilities, Nitro, Jump & Wall-Drive
docs.text: Status (verified against code 2026-07-04): nitro and jump are BUILT and live, charges, cooldowns, VFX, inputs all wired. Wall-drive is fully coded but switched off: its setup() call is commented out in car.gd:334, re-enabling and testing is a backlog quick win. Also built but never documented here: flamethrower, 4-tube rocket pot, rocket thrust, and drift (also switched off, car.gd:329). This page stays as the design reference.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
Give the driver active abilities beyond steering and throttle. These make the driver role more engaging and open up level design possibilities (jumps over gaps, wall-driving sections, speed gates).

Can be unlocked via CAR_MOD items from the shop or available from the start, decide later.

docs.title: 1. Nitro Boost
docs.text:
What: Press a key for a short burst of extra speed. Forward force on top of normal engine power for ~1.5 seconds.

Why: An active "go faster" button for dodging, ramming through barricades, clearing gaps, or escaping swarms. Creates "hit nitro and punch through" moments. Enables speed-gated shortcuts.

Behavior:

  • Charge system: start with 3 charges, each boost costs 1. Slow regen (or refill from pickups).
  • Minimum speed required (no boosting from standstill).
  • Cooldown between boosts.
  • Feedback: FOV increase, speed lines, exhaust flame VFX, boost sound.

Input: Left Shift.

docs.title: 2. Jump
docs.text:
What: The car launches upward with an impulse. Works when at least 2 wheels are grounded.

Why: Gaps to jump, obstacles to clear, enemies to jump past. Combined with nitro: "boost + jump" moments. Existing suspension handles the landing naturally.

Behavior:

  • Upward impulse + slight forward bias scaled by current speed.
  • Grounded-only (wheel contact check).
  • Single charge with cooldown, upgradeable to multiple.
  • VFX: dust burst under wheels; landing triggers camera shake.

Input: ✅ Resolved - shipped on Q. The tap-Space-jump / hold-Space-handbrake idea was dropped in favour of a dedicated key.

docs.title: 3. Wall Driving (Gravity Shift)
docs.text:
What: The car temporarily drives on walls and ceilings. Gravity is redirected to point into whatever surface the car is on.

Why: The coolest level design tool of the three. Tunnel sections where the road curves up the wall onto the ceiling. Unique visual moments; makes the tunnel feel dynamic. Also a co-op moment, the first time it happens, both players react.

Why gravity shift (not sticky wheels): Jolt vehicle wheels only generate force along the body's local down axis. Redirecting gravity so "down" points into the wall makes the wheels work naturally, no custom wheel physics. Fallback if Jolt lacks per-body gravity direction: disable gravity on the car and apply a manual force toward the surface each frame.

Behavior:

  • Activated by driving onto marked wall-drive zones (triggers in the tunnel), not a button press.
  • Surface-tracking rays detect the surface normal; gravity direction rotates smoothly to match.
  • Zone-based duration (active while in the zone) or fuel meter.
  • Leaving the surface: gravity transitions back to world-down; car launches off with current velocity.
  • Camera follows the reorientation, needs smooth up-vector interpolation.

Risks:

  • Jolt per-body gravity support needs testing.
  • Wheel behavior under redirected gravity needs hands-on verification.
  • Camera sickness from rapid orientation changes, transition speed needs tuning.
  • Wall surfaces must be smooth and wide enough for the car.

docs.title: Build order
docs.text:
1. Nitro, simplest. Forward force, charge drain, cooldown. VFX after core feel is right.
  2. Jump, upward impulse, ground check. Test landings on existing suspension.
  3. Nitro + jump VFX/audio.
  4. Wall-drive prototype, test gravity redirection on a simple ramp. Verify Jolt compatibility.
  5. Wall-drive zones, triggers in the tunnel with ramp geometry.
  6. Wall-drive camera, smooth reorientation without motion sickness.
  7. Wall-drive VFX, gravity distortion, surface glow.
  8. Shop integration, CAR_MOD items that unlock jump and wall-drive.

docs.title: Environmental Hazards
docs.text: Status: planned, confirmed NOT built (code audit 2026-07-04). None of the six hazards exist in code; junk_barricade and locked_gate are destructible obstacles, not hazards.

docs.title: What
docs.text: The tunnel itself is dangerous, not just the enemies. Falling rocks, steam vents, flooded sections, fire patches, collapsing floors, swinging obstacles. Hand-placed in the level editor at specific points.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Without hazards, the breather sections between encounters are just empty road. Hazards give the driver something meaningful to do when no enemies are around, and they combine with enemies during combat: fighting chasers while crawling through a flooded section is more interesting than either challenge alone. The tunnel feels alive instead of being a static backdrop.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
• Readable, every hazard telegraphs BEFORE it hits. No unfair surprise damage.
  • Driver-focused, hazards test reactions and path choice. The gunner can sometimes help (shoot a stalactite early), but driving skill is the main defense.
  • Non-lethal individually, one hazard does moderate damage or disrupts movement. Danger comes from combinations.

docs.title: Hazard roster
docs.text: Hazard Challenge type Tell Falling rocks Speed rewards, outrun the drop. Gunner can pre-trigger stalactites. Falling dust, pebbles ~0.5s early, cracking sound Steam vents Pattern reading, 2s on / 3s off cycle; push force + light damage Hiss + glow 1s before blast Flooded section Momentum, drag and traction loss; deep water stalls the engine. Rewards commitment, punishes hesitation Visible water, shallow -> deep Fire patches Path choice, fires don't cover the full width; pick a line. Tick damage Fire + orange wall glow, crackling Collapsing floor Speed check, fast clears the gap, slow falls in. "Hit nitro NOW" moments Floor cracks, cracking audio, crumbles edge-in Swinging obstacles Timing, pendulums across the tunnel; blast through between swings or wait Always visible + creaking metal

docs.title: Placement philosophy
docs.text:
• Breather sections: 1-2 hazards as the only challenge. Driver-focused, no enemies.
  • During encounters: lighter hazards (one vent, one fire patch) combined with enemies.
  • Boss fight: the boss can trigger hazards as attack patterns (laser ignites fuel barrels; slams collapse floor).

docs.title: Build order
docs.text:
1. Steam vent, simplest (timer cycle, push, tick damage)
  2. Fire patch
  3. Falling rocks
  4. Flooded zone (drag/friction modifiers)
  5. Collapsing floor
  6. Swinging obstacles
  7. Combine with enemy spawns in the tunnel
  8. Boss hazard integration

docs.title: Run Modifiers
docs.text: Status: planned, confirmed NOT built (code audit 2026-07-04). Lower priority: build after the core loop is solid.

docs.title: What
docs.text: Before a run, players pick modifiers that change the rules: harder for better rewards, different playstyles, or pure chaos. Think Hades' Pact of Punishment or Risk of Rain 2's artifacts. Both players vote in the hub before departing, a pre-run negotiation moment ("do we take the hard modifier for double loot?") that reinforces the co-op dynamic.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Without modifiers, every run plays the same. Risk/reward trade-offs give skilled players a reason to push themselves; the voting creates co-op discussion before the run even starts.

docs.title: Difficulty modifiers (risk/reward)
docs.text: Modifier Effect Currency bonus Tough Horde Enemies +50% HP +30% Swarm 50% more enemies per spawn +40% Fragile Ride Car -30% max HP +25% No Brakes Minimum speed enforced (auto-accelerate to 30 km/h) +20% Dark Tunnels More fog, fewer lights +25% Aggro 2x detection range, more frequent attacks +35%

docs.title: Playstyle modifiers
docs.text: Modifier Effect Speed Demon Top speed +40%, braking -50% Glass Cannon Turret damage x2, car damage taken x1.5 Scavenger No shop this run, loot drops x3 Bomber Run Start with 5 bombs; enemies drop bombs instead of loot Heavyweight Car mass x2, tankier, slower, devastating rams

docs.title: Fun / chaos modifiers
docs.text: Modifier Effect Low Gravity Gravity 60%, car floats on bumps, enemies bounce Big Head Mode Enemy meshes x2, easier to hit, looks ridiculous Turbo Fuse All explosions x3 radius (theirs AND yours) Ricochet Turret bullets bounce off walls once Earthquake Random shake + physics impulses every 10-20s

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
• Modifiers are data flags, not code injections. Each is a resource file with an ID; game systems check the flag and adjust their own behavior. No central "apply all modifiers" function.
  • Adding a modifier = one resource file + one check in the affected system.
  • Conflicts declared per modifier (e.g. Speed Demon x No Brakes), selecting one greys out the other.
  • Progressive unlock: some modifiers appear only after N completed runs; chaos modifiers after the first boss kill.

docs.title: Selection flow
docs.text: Terminal in the hub. Grid of modifier cards by category. Either player toggles; both see changes live (server-authoritative). Total currency multiplier shown. Both must press Ready to depart, no forced consensus, just communication.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text: Unlock state saves to disk. Active modifiers reset every run, set in hub, synced to peers, cleared at run end.

docs.title: Build order
docs.text:
1. RunModifier resource + ModifierManager autoload
  2. One modifier end-to-end (Tough Horde)
  3. Currency multiplier
  4. Selection UI
  5. Voting/ready system
  6. Remaining difficulty modifiers -> playstyle -> fun
  7. Unlock progression
  8. Combo rewards (cosmetics for hard combos)

docs.title: Shortcut Paths
docs.text: Status: planned, confirmed NOT built (code audit 2026-07-04). No chest, no timed gate, no ping system; junk_barricade/locked_gate exist but as main-path obstacles, not optional side paths.

docs.title: What
docs.text: Hidden side tunnels branching off the main path, behind destructible walls, speed-gated, or dead-ending in treasure rooms. Shortcuts reward exploration and co-op awareness: the driver watches the road while the gunner scans the walls.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Without shortcuts, the tunnel is a linear corridor and every run feels the same. Shortcuts give players a reason to look around, create risk/reward decisions, reward the non-driving player for paying attention, and add replayability through discovery.

docs.title: 1. Destructible Wall Shortcut
docs.text:
A side tunnel hidden behind a cracked wall. The gunner (or a bomb) shoots it open. Co-op discovery moment: gunner spots the cracks, opens it, calls it out; driver commits.

Unlike barricades (which block the main path and must be dealt with), these are optional and easy to miss. Lower HP, a quick turret burst opens them.

Cues: light seeping through cracks, trickling dust, subtly different texture, wind whistling.

docs.title: 2. Timed Shortcut (Speed Gate)
docs.text:
A side path whose gate is open for a limited time, a trigger zone ahead starts the timer. Fast enough (nitro helps) and you make it. Rewards maintained speed and split-second commitment.

Cues: wall lights flash a "this way" sequence, gate color (green open / red closing), loud mechanical close.

docs.title: 3. Hidden Room (Dead End with Loot)
docs.text: A small chamber off the main tunnel with a chest, the car doesn't fit; someone walks in on foot. Camouflaged entrance (waterfall, dark alcove, hanging debris), warm golden glow inside. Creates a "stop the car?" decision while enemies could be catching up. Prime drone-scouting content.

docs.title: 4. Risk/Reward Fork
docs.text:
An open fork: main path = enemy encounter, side path = hazard gauntlet. Both reconnect after the same distance. Lets the team pick their challenge type, the hazard path plays to the driver's strengths. Loot chests reward the harder driving route.

No new systems needed, it's a level-design pattern using existing spawns and hazards.

docs.title: Discovery & co-op interaction
docs.text:
• Gunner: wider, elevated field of view, naturally scans walls while tracking enemies. Pings discovered shortcuts for the driver.
  • Drone: flies into spaces the car can't reach, scouts ahead, shows the passenger hidden entrances.
  • Driver: makes the call, take it or stay on the main path. Timed gates demand instant reaction; wall shortcuts allow discussion while the gunner shoots.

docs.title: Placement philosophy
docs.text:
• Skip shortcuts branch off before an encounter and reconnect after, skipping it means less currency but more safety.
  • Loot shortcuts branch off breather sections and dead-end with treasure.
  • Risk/reward forks replace an encounter with a choice: fight or hazard run.
  • Untriggered spawn zones never spawn, shortcuts genuinely skip content, no wasted resources.

docs.title: Build order
docs.text:
1. Destructible wall shortcut (reuse barricade system + cues)
  2. Interactable chest (opens on E, loot table, server-authoritative)
  3. Loot room
  4. Timed shortcut gate
  5. Risk/reward fork (level design only, no new code)
  6. Discovery cue polish
  7. Drone scouting (works via existing camera)

docs.title: Music & Soundtrack
docs.text: Status: direction only, nothing built. This is the music page - separate from the technical engine audio system, which is about how the car sounds, not what plays over (or in) the game.

docs.title: The vibe
docs.text: Old-school synthwave rock - synth-driven but with real guitar bite and drive. Tentative, but the touchstone is Dance with the Dead (darksynth + heavy guitar): propulsive, retro, a little menacing. It fits the premise - an anachronistic muscle car tearing through a dead megastructure wants exactly that energy.

docs.title: The car radio (music is diegetic)
docs.text:
The core decision: music plays from an in-car radio the crew switches on - it lives in the world, not laid over the top (decision). It's the same logic as the engine being the co-op living room's soundtrack and the colour being diegetic: plugged in = alive = the radio comes on.

Why it earns its place:

  • Reinforces the premise - the car is power, life, colour, and the music. It's all one thing switching on.
  • It's controllable in-fiction: the crew turns it up, changes the station, kills it. Music becomes something players do, not just hear.
  • Room to play: does the radio duck or cut in tense moments? Does deep-zone interference chew at the signal like the drone tether? All on the table.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Where and when does music play? Hub only, whole runs, combat stingers, or sparse ambient in the tunnels? Undecided - and it collides with pacing: the tunnels' target feel is headlights through fog, which may want tense ambient, not a banging track. Music placement and the scale-dread pacing have to be designed together (The Tunnels).
  • All radio, or radio + score? Pure diegetic radio, or a non-diegetic score too for set-pieces like a landmark fight?
  • Original vs licensed. Synthwave-rock references imply real artists; originals in that style keep licensing clean.

docs.title: Other (non-engine) sound
docs.text: The turret, screech, explosion, and UI SFX still predate the engine-audio system and use one-shot players - a consistency and bus-routing pass is a someday (engine audio open items, backlog). This page will grow to hold the whole sound direction as it firms up.
